artifact.title: Technical Report: The Architectural Role of Redb in High-Frequency Agentic Systems

authority.authority_path: published Node package -> napi-rs native binding -> local Rust crate implementation

runtime_proof.sona_learning: Forced learning: 1 trajectories -> 1 patterns, status: completed

negative_findings.agentdb_feedback: recordFeedback called with the documented search hit ID did not increment trajectoriesRecorded because SelfLearningRvfBackend expects an internal q_* ID that search does not return.

agentdb_witness.error: this.db.verifyWitness is not a function. (In 'this.db.verifyWitness()', 'this.db.verifyWitness' is undefined)

negative_findings.rvf_combined_payload: The live AgentDB RVF contained manifest/vector/witness segments; it did not prove persisted Micro-LoRA plus FastGRNN segments in the same file.

interpretation.supported: Measured redb reads and Immediate commits are in the sub-microsecond/microsecond class on this hardware.
interpretation.not_supported: No LMDB or PostgreSQL comparator was measured; cosine is not redb and AVX-512 dispatch was not demonstrated.

claims.conclusion: redb 4.1.0 README and 196 passing upstream library/integration tests prove a pure-Rust embedded key-value library with no database daemon or sidecar. This verdict is about redb itself, not the larger dynamically linked LifeOS closure.
primary_pointers: redb-4.1.0 README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/design.md, src/db.rs, src/types.rs and 196-test run

claims.conclusion: Current pinned redb 4.1.0 removed its mmap backend. Its optimized file backend uses positioned file reads/writes, and Database exposes a configurable userspace cache (default 1 GiB). The report's current mmap and operating-system-cache-bypass statement is false.

claims.conclusion: redb documents a zero-copy, BTreeMap-based API and can return borrowed views, but its Value trait explicitly serializes with as_bytes and deserializes with from_bytes. Storage uses copy-on-write B-trees, not std::collections::BTreeMap, so 'native Rust types without serialization' is too broad.

claims.conclusion: redb 4.1.0 documents full ACID transactions and serializable isolation; the complete local redb test suite passed 196 tests including immediate persistence, read isolation, transaction behavior, recovery-sensitive paths, and multithreading.

claims.conclusion: redb 4.1.0 documents MVCC with one writer and multiple concurrent snapshot readers; design documentation and passing concurrency tests prove readers do not block the active writer. Writer-to-writer concurrency remains serialized.

claims.conclusion: The pinned artifact is a Rust library crate linked into applications. It is not a compiler and exposes embedded database APIs.

claims.conclusion: redb's Rust implementation is compatible with a musl/static target in principle, but portability is a whole-closure property. The active CodeDB/Yazelix binaries are dynamically linked glibc ELF files with Nix-store interpreter/RUNPATH entries, and the effective Rust toolchain lacked the musl std target, so the claimed portable closure is not currently proven.

claims.conclusion: redb 4.1.0 is a transactional key-value B-tree engine. It exposes no vector coordinate, geometry, nearest-neighbor, HNSW, or distance-computation API. The current Bun-loaded ruvector Node package owns vector geometry.

claims.conclusion: redb implements no cosine-similarity operation. Cosine is supplied by application or RuVector code; the Bun-loaded ruvector 0.2.34 package exports cosineSimilarity and native vector database APIs.

claims.conclusion: The exact zero-copy-f32-plus-AVX-512 redb path does not exist in redb 4.1.0. A reproducible benchmark measured redb byte reads and commits plus a separate scalar application cosine loop at 384 and 4096 dimensions; microsecond results do not convert that application loop into redb functionality or prove AVX-512 use.

claims.conclusion: The component boundary is correct: embedding models create semantic vectors while redb passively stores and retrieves caller-supplied encoded values. Distance computation and semantic meaning remain external.

claims.conclusion: redb is an in-process single-node embedded store and exposes no network server, replication protocol, or distributed-consensus API. Distribution therefore belongs to application protocols.
primary_pointers: redb-4.1.0 README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/design.md, src/db.rs, src/types.rs and 196-test run

claims.conclusion: redb is key-value and has no SQL or automatic relational secondary-index facility. Qualification is required because it supports named tables, range scans, and multimap tables from which an application can explicitly build indexes.
primary_pointers: redb-4.1.0 README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/design.md, src/db.rs, src/types.rs and 196-test run

claims.conclusion: The local benchmark proves low redb latency on this workstation (1536-byte read p50 about 0.27 microseconds; Immediate commit p50 about 20-24 microseconds), but no claim-scoped LMDB comparator, edge-node hardware run, determinism definition, or crash/sync matrix exists. Competitive-with-LMDB remains unproved.

claims.conclusion: redb can technically implement an embedded high-frequency scratchpad, and measured local operations are fast enough for that use class. No accepted LifeOS data-class, retention, privacy, promotion, or recovery decision makes this its current exclusive role.

claims.conclusion: redb can store an embedding under a BLAKE3-derived key, so the mechanism is feasible. No current model-aware cache schema or implementation proves that content, model, version, configuration, invalidation, and collision handling are all covered.

claims.conclusion: redb can back a durable application outbox or replay queue, but it is not PostgreSQL WAL and no current ordered/idempotent redb-to-PostgreSQL outage protocol is implemented. WAL, outbox, queue, buffer, and authority semantics must not be conflated.

claims.conclusion: Current CodeDB Rust code can transform supported source files into structured syntax rows and Nushell can host those rows. It is not a universal Nushell-owned AST transformer: language coverage varies and Cargo dependency rows come from cargo metadata rather than the Rust AST.

claims.conclusion: At canonical nu_plugin origin/master 9451b20, the Rust plugin uses the Nushell MessagePack protocol but returns typed table values and has no direct redb dependency. Durable redb capture is a separate codedb CLI/BlobStore path, so no direct plugin-MessagePack-to-redb stream exists.

claims.conclusion: An intermediate redb ingestion buffer is a technically possible target, but it is not the universal current role. Current CodeDB uses redb as a durable default source-blob store and envctl uses redb separately for migration/state; authority must be decided per data class.

claims.conclusion: envctl commit c911286 reads exported JSONL rows that already contain embedding_minilm vectors into an in-memory RuVector index. It neither pulls CodeDB redb blocks, generates MiniLM embeddings, nor commits code and vectors to PostgreSQL.

claims.conclusion: Reproducible local evidence supports the redb microsecond tier for the measured byte-read and Immediate-commit operations. No equivalent PostgreSQL-plus-RuVector relational/GNN benchmark was run, and Bun-loaded ruvector itself exposes native sub-millisecond vector operations, so the report's global microsecond-versus-millisecond boundary is not established.

claims.conclusion: RuVector includes a native pgrx PostgreSQL extension that installs with CREATE EXTENSION and needs no vector-database sidecar. RuVector is broader than that extension: the current developer-facing ruvector 0.2.34 Node package loaded a standalone native N-API vector database through Bun, and no installed LifeOS PostgreSQL extension activation was proven.

claims.conclusion: Current envctl does not select PostgreSQL branch state or reconstruct files from module_path. Its optional semantic adapter treats module_path as vector identity/metadata; current materialization is a separate CodeDB operation.

claims.conclusion: redb is passive transactional storage and has no ranking or learning semantics. Any ranking change is owned by application, model, RuVector, SONA, or AgentDB code.

claims.conclusion: The latest developer-facing AgentDB package proves the core integration: agentdb 3.0.0-alpha.17 exposes SelfLearningRvfBackend over .rvf, wraps @ruvector/sona, enhances searches, records trajectories, accepts quality feedback, trains patterns, and a Bun live probe created an AgentDB .rvf plus native SONA learning. Qualification is required because search does not expose the internal q_* trajectory ID expected by recordFeedback; the documented hit-ID call recorded zero trajectories in the live probe, so the complete feedback-to-ranking contract is currently defective.
primary_pointers: redb-4.1.0 README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/design.md, src/db.rs, src/types.rs and 196-test run

claims.conclusion: The latest Bun-loaded packages prove every narrower component: RVF defines model/overlay/LoRA segments, native SONA exposes Micro-LoRA, ruvllm held and switched 51 in-memory LoRA adapters, and Tiny Dancer trained and scored a FastGRNN router. They do not prove that one current AgentDB .rvf file persisted both the Micro-LoRA adapters and FastGRNN model, nor that 50+ complete ruvllm personalities hot-swap within one measured hardware footprint.
primary_pointers: redb-4.1.0 README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/design.md, src/db.rs, src/types.rs and 196-test run

claims.conclusion: The defensible boundary is verified: redb can be one portable embedded storage component but is not a geometry engine, vector database, learning system, SQL authority, or complete intelligence architecture. Its elevation to the strategic LifeOS foundation remains an unrecorded owner architecture decision.
